content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 13, 2011


Renee A. Stadel
Deputy City Attorney
City of Los Angeles
City Hall East
200 N. Main Street
Los Angeles, CA 90012

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-11-040

Dear Ms. Stadel:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of members of the City of Los
Angeles Employee Relations Board (the "ERB") regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we base our advice solely on the
provisions of the Act. We do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest
laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. Furthermore,
we do not address the applicability, if any, of the provisions of your agency's statement of
incompatible activities.

QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS

1. Is a union local that is a party in a matter pending before the ERB (the "ERB union")
and was a party in a private arbitration in which an ERB member was paid an arbitration fee a
source of income to the ERB member if:

(a) The union local paid all or part of the ERB member's arbitration fee?

Yes. The arbitration fee paid to the ERB member is income. Because the local paid fees
aggregating $500 or more to the ERB member within 12 months of a governmental decision that
will be made by the ERB member, the local is a source of income.

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-11-040
Page No. 2

(b) The employer that was a party in the arbitration paid the arbitration fee?

No. Because the employer paid the fee, the employer is the source of income.

2. Is a union local that is not involved in the ERB matter (the "uninvolved local") a source of income to the ERB member if:

(a) The uninvolved local paid all or part of the arbitration fee and both the uninvolved local and the ERB local are affiliated with the same umbrella organization?

No. The two locals operate separately and independently. Their affiliation with the same umbrella organization does not make the local involved in the arbitration a source of income to an official making decisions in the unrelated ERB matter.

(b) The uninvolved local paid all or part of the arbitration fee and staff or attorneys of the umbrella organization assisted or represented the uninvolved local in the arbitration and currently represent a different ERB local in the unrelated ERB matter?

No. The attorney acts on behalf of the uninvolved local when the attorney represents it in an arbitration and pays the arbitration fee. Because the arbitration and the ERB matter involve separate and independent locals in unrelated matters, the local involved in the arbitration is not a source of income to the ERB member.

(c) The umbrella organization that currently represents the ERB local in the ERB matter paid the arbitration fee in an unrelated arbitration involving the uninvolved local?

No. The umbrella organization acted on behalf of the uninvolved local when the umbrella organization paid the arbitration fee. Because the arbitration and the ERB matter involve separate and independent locals in unrelated matters, the local involved in the arbitration is not a source of income to the ERB member.

(d) The attorney representing the ERB local in the ERB matter previously represented the uninvolved local in an unrelated arbitration and the attorney paid the arbitration fee?

No. The umbrella organization's staff or attorneys acted on behalf of the uninvolved local in the arbitration. Because the arbitration and the ERB matter involve separate and independent locals in unrelated matters, the umbrella organization is not a source of income to the ERB member.

(3) Is the umbrella organization that paid the arbitration fee a source of income if the arbitration and the ERB matters involve different locals?

No. The umbrella organization paid the arbitration fee on behalf of the uninvolved local. Because the arbitration and the ERB matters involve separate and independent locals in unrelated matters, the umbrella organization is not a source of income to the ERB member.

File No. A-11-040
Page No. 3

(4) Is an attorney who represented a local in an arbitration and paid the arbitration fee a
source of income if the attorney represents a different local in an unrelated ERB matter?

No. Because the attorney represents separate and independent locals in unrelated
matters, the attorney is not a source of income to the ERB member.

FACTS

The City of Los Angeles has adopted an employee relations ordinance establishing the
ERB. ERB members make decisions to determine employee representation units, determine the
validity of charges of unfair employee relations practices and act upon requests for mediation or
fact finding in connection with resolution of impasses. The ERB shares some similarities with
the state Public Employment Relations Board. The ERB is the final decision maker on matters
within its jurisdiction, and its decisions are subject to court review.

Current matters pending before the ERB include: an unfair employee relations claim filed
by AFSCME Local 3090, SEIU Local 721, L/OC County Business Trades, Operating Engineers
Local 501, Teamsters Local 911 and Laborers Local 777 alleging that the city failed to fully
respond to an information request regarding unit employee layoffs and furloughs; an unfair
employee relations claim filed by SEIU Local 721 against the City Administrative Officer
alleging an absence of good faith bargaining; and a request for consideration of impasse
procedures by the City Administrative Officer on a successor Memorandum of Understanding
with SEIU Local 721 regarding two MOUs where SEIU 721 has filed a notice of contention. In
addition, the Coalition of City Unions, composed of six labor organizations representing city
employees, has filed matters with the ERB.

In various circumstances, a labor organization or its representative might be involved in
an ERB matter and a private arbitration. Three current Board members provide their services as
arbitrators in private arbitrations for which they are paid an arbitration fee. In most arbitrations,
the parties, including the local, pay these fees.

The arbitrations often involve local labor organizations that represent city employees, as
well as international, national or district labor organizations with which the locals are affiliated
(the "umbrella organizations"). By way of example, SEIU 721 is a local that is affiliated with
the international labor organization SEIU; AFSCME local No. 3090 is affiliated with AFSCME
District Council 36; and particular local trade unions, such as the pipefitters' union, are affiliated
with the Los Angeles and Orange County Building Trades Council.

Your questions relate to a potential source-of-income conflict of interest when a Board
member has been paid an arbitration fee.

File No. A-11-040
Page No. 4

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform their duties
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them." A public official may not make, participate in
making or in any way use or attempt to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to know that he or she has a
disqualifying conflict of interest. (Section 87100; Regulation 18700(a).) Per your request, we
limit our analysis to determining whether an individual or organization that pays an arbitration
fee to an ERB member is a source of income to the member.

A public official has a source-of-income economic interest in any person from whom the
official received income aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the time the official
makes the relevant governmental decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) "Income"
is defined broadly in Section 82030 and would include arbitration fees received by a Board
member. For purposes of our analysis, we assume that each arbitration fee was $500 or more
and was received by the Board member within 12 months prior to the member making a decision
in the ERB matter.

In correspondence subsequent to your request for advice, you and I discussed breaking
down your questions into multiple questions to clarify the facts. In an attempt to simplify our
analysis, we have since consolidated and rephrased those questions. In responding to each
rephrased question, we are including footnote references to the questions listed in your
March 31, 2011 communication. We have also separated the analysis as to arbitrations and ERB
matters involving the same party and those involving different locals.

The same local is involved in the arbitration and the ERB matter.

Question 1: Is a local that is a party in a matter pending before the ERB and was a party in a
private arbitration in which an ERB member was paid an arbitration fee a source of income to
the ERB member if:

(a) The local paid all or part of the ERB member's arbitration fee?²

The arbitration fee paid to the ERB member is income. Under our assumed facts,
the local paid arbitration fees of $500 or more to the ERB member within 12 months of the ERB
member's decision. Accordingly, the local is a source of income to the ERB member.

(b) The employer that was a party in the arbitration paid the arbitration fee?³

No. Because the employer paid the fee, the employer is the source of income.

_________________
² Question 6b
³ Questions 4a, 6a

File No. A-11-040
Page No. 5

The local that was a party in the arbitration is not involved in the ERB matter.

Question 2. Is a union local that is not involved in the ERB matter a source of income to the ERB member if:

(a) The uninvolved local paid all or part of the arbitration fee and both the uninvolved local and the ERB local are affiliated with the same umbrella organization. 4

You have indicated that the two locals operate separately and independently. In prior advice we have said that the affiliation of two locals with the same umbrella organization does not make one local a source of income to an official making decisions involving a different local. (Barisone Advice Letter, No. I-08-176.) Accordingly, the uninvolved local is not a source of income to the official making decisions in the unrelated ERB matter.

(b) The uninvolved local paid all or part of the arbitration fee and staff or attorneys of the umbrella organization assisted or represented the uninvolved local in the arbitration and currently represent the ERB local in the unrelated ERB matter?5

The attorney acts on behalf of the local when the attorney represents a local in an arbitration and pays the arbitration fee. Because the arbitration and the ERB matter involve separate and independent locals in unrelated matters, the local involved in the arbitration is not a source of income to the ERB member.

(c) The umbrella organization that currently represents the ERB local in the ERB matter paid the arbitration fee in an unrelated arbitration involving a different local?6

We have previously advised that a party in an arbitration proceeding, not the American Association of Arbitration, is the source of income to a public official even though the AAA paid the arbitration fee. We reached a similar result where an insurance carrier made payments on behalf of an insured. (See Barbosa Advice Letter No. I-91-365, Olson Advice Letter No. I-93-259, Gordon Advice Letter, No. A-09-240; Burns Advice Letter, No. I-90-328; Morris Advice Letter, No. I-90-373.) Also, we have advised that a customer, not the payer, is the source of income where the customer has the legal obligation to pay. (McLean Advice Letter, No. A-93-339 and Kolkey Advice Letter, No. A-95-14.)

It is our understanding that an umbrella organization is acting on behalf of a local (or a group of locals) when it is a party in an arbitration or ERB matter. If the local, rather than the umbrella organization, is considered the source of income, the same analysis used in our answer to question 2(a) applies. Because the two locals operate separately and independently, the local involved in the arbitration is not a source of income to the ERB member.

___________________
4 Questions 1
5 Question 3a
6 Question 2a

File No. A-11-040
Page No. 6

(d) The attorney representing the ERB local in the ERB matter previously represented
the uninvolved local in an unrelated arbitration and the attorney paid the arbitration fee?7

Generally, when an attorney pays an arbitration fee in a private arbitration, the attorney
does so as part of his or her representation of the client and the payment is, therefore, made on
behalf of the client. Accordingly, the attorney paid the arbitration fee on behalf of the
uninvolved local. Because the arbitration and the ERB matters involve locals that operate
separately and independently, neither the uninvolved local nor the attorney is a source of income
to the ERB member.

Question 3: Is the umbrella organization that paid the arbitration fee a source of income
if the arbitration and the ERB matters involve different locals?8

As stated above, the umbrella organization paid the arbitration fee on behalf of the
uninvolved local. Because the arbitration and the ERB matters involve locals that operate
separately and independently, the umbrella organization is not a source of income to the ERB
member.

Question 4: Is an attorney who represented a local in an arbitration and paid the
arbitration fee a source income if the attorney represents the ERB local in an unrelated ERB
matter?9

As stated above, an attorney who pays the arbitration fee in a private arbitration makes
the payment on behalf of the client. Because the attorney was representing different locals in
unrelated matters, the attorney is not a source of income to the ERB member.

If you have other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl

7 Question 4b
8 Questions 2b, 3b, 5
9 Question 4c

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform their duties

making or in any way use or attempt to use his or her official position to influence a

The same local is involved in the arbitration and the ERB matter.
